outliers.transcript: McKinley and I were out for dinner recently and the guy on the table next to us about halfway through the dinner leans over and compliments me for my watch. I was wearing my Everose Gold Rolex Day-Date, which I bought as a dinner watch, special occasion watch, but it's very quickly become my daily driver. He showed me his watch. He was wearing a Pikachu Daytona with an Oysterflex, for any of you who are watch nerds here. And so we started chatting and the conversation quickly devolved, as it normally does, into cars, travel, watches, wine, all of the things that I like to do. Eventually, we began to talk about business and we quickly realized that we're kind of in the same industry, similar industries, at least. And we began to talk about numbers and revenue and the things that we were doing. This guy was doing between 350 to $450,000 a month in monthly revenue. And when I told him that I was doing between 80 and $100,000 a month in monthly recurring revenue, he was shocked. And the reason he was shocked is because he couldn't believe the things that I was able to own with the revenue numbers I was at. And I'd begin to wager that the reason he was so shocked is because I'm keeping a lot more of my 80 to 100 than he is keeping of his 350 to 450. I would actually suspect that I'm keeping more profit each month than he is keeping, even though he's doing three to four times the revenue that I'm doing per month. See, the business you build is only as good as the profit you keep. And if you have no profit, you have no freedom. And most of you are building a business because you want freedom. Whereas instead, if you're focused only on revenue, you'll quickly devolve into having a giant team, giant ad spend, and zero freedom because you're not keeping much of what you make each month. If as your revenue goes up, your life goes down, you, my friend, have made a bad trade because the actual goal that you wanna achieve is full bank account, empty calendar. You wanna be able to run a business that fills your bank account and still allows you to travel, own the things you want, and spend the time doing what you wanna do. I right now, I'm in beautiful Queenstown, New Zealand for a week on a trip that was reasonably unplanned. We booked the flights about four days before we got on the plane. And so that is only possible because I've built the business that allows me to do that. You can build a great business and live a good life. You just have to be intentional about it.

outliers.source_caption:
Public apology:⁠
⁠
For years, I taught that once your offer went over $2k… you needed a sales call.⁠
⁠
Under $2k? Sure, sell it with a doc, a webinar, whatever.⁠
⁠
Over $2k? “Better get the closers in.”⁠
⁠
Turns out… that was complete rubbish.⁠
⁠
A client of mine had a $30k offer being sold in chat by VAs with their thumbs… while the “real” sales team was busy having feelings about it.⁠
⁠
That was the moment the penny dropped.⁠
⁠
If someone can sell a $30k offer without a call…⁠
⁠
maybe the call wasn’t the magic.⁠
⁠
Maybe the system was broken.⁠
⁠
So we tested it.⁠
⁠
Locked the sales team away.⁠
⁠
No calls.⁠
⁠
No chats.⁠
⁠
No closing.⁠
⁠
Nothing.⁠
⁠
15 months later?⁠
⁠
We’re selling 5x more every month than we did with a full sales team.⁠
⁠
That’s Salesless™.⁠
⁠
Comment SALESLESS for the deets.
outliers.transcript: I lied to you and I need to make a public apology. For years, I taught my clients that if you were selling something for under two grand, you didn't need a sales call. You could sell it with a doc or a webinar or a whatever. But at the moment, your price point crossed to $2,001. Oh, now we need a sales call. What an idiot I was. That was dead wrong and I'm really sorry. Let me tell you how I realized that was a complete lie. I've got this great client called Jackson. He's got two programs. One's just under two grand and one's 30K. This one was sold by a virtual assistant in chat and this one had a big fat sales team of closers trying to crush deals. One day, Jackson gets pulled into a meeting because the sales team is upset at the virtual assistants. You know why? Because the VAs in the Philippines were closing $30,000 deals with their thumbs in chat without getting on the phone. That's not fair. They're our leads. Jackson saw it first. Well, if someone can sell it without a call, I don't need you guys anymore. That planted the seed that became an incredible experiment. Probably the best test that I've run in the last couple of years. 15 months ago, we took our sales team, we locked them in a cupboard and told them, do not talk to anybody under any circumstances. We wanted to see if we could sell a $30,000 or a $60,000 program with no calls. Turns out you kind of can. So for the last 15 months, we've had nobody in sales, no chats, no closes, no nothing. We're selling five times more every single month than we ever did with a big fat sales team trying to book appointments. It started as an experiment for us, but now it's turned into a system that we've used with hundreds of clients who are now all signing high-ticket clients every single week with no one in sales, no sales calls required. We call it salesless. So it's worked for me, it's worked for our clients, it'll work for you. If you comment the word sales below, I want to send you something cool that we just hooked up to help you do the same.

outliers.source_caption: Make sure you know what you’re working for 🫡
outliers.transcript: I've had this arbitrary goal of making a million dollars in a month for a long time now. And I had this funny experience recently where I began to realize that that might be a bit of a stupid goal. I did this exercise just the other day that was designed to figure out exactly what my ceiling number is. What's the amount of money I need to make in my business in terms of revenue, so then taxes, expenses, everything is paid, and what I keep afterwards allows me to live a life beyond my wildest dreams. I'm not talking what I'm doing now. I'm not even talking a little bit better. I'm talking excessively good lifestyle. What is that number? Now on this list was increasing our rent by three times and finding a ridiculous house, my 911 GT3, McEly's Cayenne Turbo GT, a $150,000 a year watch fund that I'm gonna spend, a $200,000 a year travel fund, a $100,000 a year luxury gifts fund, a twice weekly cleaner, a personal chef, and all of that while still maintaining reasonably healthy profit margins of 75% and maintaining a personal saving and investing rate of 50% of everything we make. And so whatever I spend on this crazy lifestyle, exactly that same amount every single month needs to be saved and invested. And what was my number? $278,000 a month in revenue, which may sound like a big number until you compare it to a million dollars a month. And suddenly I've got about $720,000 of unnecessary revenue that I would be making every single month that is just making me work harder for no personal gain. And a really funny thing happened once I finally realized that my ceiling number is actually a lot lower than I expected. Suddenly I'm having more fun in my business because I'm no longer in such a rush to get to this arbitrary goal that actually wouldn't lead to any other considerable change in my life. See, I think most of you have just set a goal and never actually thought about what that goal actually means for your lifestyle. And so often you're chasing something that is so far beyond what you would ever need that it's making you rush. And when you rush, you make decisions that potentially trade off what you're actually trying to build. See, if I was pushing to a million dollars a month in revenue, what is that going to mean? It's probably going to mean I'm gonna have to do things that are going to have to sacrifice my lifestyle in the short term in order to maximize revenue. And that, in my opinion, is a bad fucking trade. I'm trying to build a business that supports my lifestyle and supports my team and fulfills me personally. And so anything beyond what supports my team and supports my lifestyle is just ego. And until you figure out what exactly that number is, you're going to be chasing something that may force you to take actions that aren't actually serving the goal. They're just serving this arbitrary idea of the goal.
outliers.why_it_worked: It taps into founder anxiety about 'big enough' goals and gives them permission to want a rich life over a rich ego, using specific, aspirational details to make the concept tangible.

outliers.headline: The Hustle Playbook is a Lie
outliers.recommended_angle: Christian's version will attack the 'hustle porn' playbook that chains founders to their desks. The angle is that the best founders aren't trying to work more; they're building systems that work for them so they can get their time and life back.
my_hooks: Quick question. Do you want to work 16 hours a day? You don't? Oh, I don't want to work 16 hours a day either.
Hot take: Your business should not need you to run.
The hustle culture playbook is dead. It just doesn't know it yet.
We've been lied to that the only way to grow is to outwork everyone. It's just not true.
2018 called. It wants its hustle porn back.
my_talking_points: Hey, quick question. Do you want to be chained to your laptop all day? You don't? I don't want to be either.
Your best life isn't found behind a screen. But we've been lied to. We've been told the only way to build a successful online business is to grind, post constantly, and be 'always on'.
It's just not true. That old hustle playbook? It doesn't build a business. It builds a prison with you as the warden.
The founders I respect most aren't grinding harder. They've already done that. They're building smarter.
They know what they're good at. They know what they want to achieve. They don't need another tactic. They need leverage.
They need systems that run without them. Engines that generate leads, nurture customers, and create content in their voice, 24/7.
There's a shift happening. You've probably felt it. The goal isn't just to build online. It's to build online so you can live offline.
This is why I created FieldWork. We install the systems that buy back your time. AI employees, automation engines, content machines.
It worked for me, then it worked for my clients. Now it could be your turn.
If you want to build a business that doesn't own your life, comment the word FIELDWORK below. I'll show you how it works.
outliers.my_body_copy: Hey, quick question. Do you want to be chained to your laptop all day? You don't? I don't want to be either. Your best life isn't found behind a screen. But we've been lied to that the only way to build a real business is to grind yourself into the ground. That old hustle playbook is broken. It doesn't build a business. It builds a prison. And you're the only inmate. The best founders I know aren't trying to work more. They're past that. They're building for leverage. They want systems. Engines that generate leads, nurture customers, and create content while they're out in the world, living. There's a shift happening. The goal isn't just to build online. It's to build online so you can live offline. That's why I created FieldWork. We don't teach you more tactics. We install the systems that buy back your time. If you want to build a business that serves your life, not the other way around, comment the word FIELDWORK below and I'll show you how.
my_script_summary: Hook: No one wants to be chained to their laptop.
Problem: The old 'hustle' playbook builds a prison, not a business.
Insight: The best founders want leverage and systems, not more work.
The Shift: The new goal is to 'Build Online. Live Offline.'
Solution: FieldWork installs the systems to achieve this.
CTA: Comment FIELDWORK to learn how.
production_guidance: Shoot as a direct-to-camera talking head. Keep the energy grounded but confident, like sharing a secret with a friend.
B-roll: Splice in quick cuts of you living 'offline'. Hiking, with your camera, in the van, time with family. Visually contrast the 'prison' with the 'field'.
On-Screen Text: Use bold, simple text to emphasize key phrases like 'It builds a prison', 'They need leverage', and the final call to action.
End Card: Hold on a final shot with the words 'Comment: FIELDWORK' clearly displayed on screen for 3-4 seconds.

outliers.relevance_reason: Personal reflection on self-doubt, no connection to FieldWork's mission.
outliers.source_caption: A part of me still doesn’t believe in myself
outliers.transcript: I'm gonna share something incredibly vulnerable in this video because this is a trauma that I am actively working through and I'm gonna share it with you, one, to hopefully help you understand me better, but two, maybe to recognize this same pattern of behavior in yourself so you too can learn from my mistakes and start to work through it yourself. I, to a large extent, do not actually believe that I am capable of achieving the success that I want to achieve. When I was growing up, I experienced a number of situations which made me believe, at least, that other kids that I was growing up around possessed ingredients or raw ingredients that I did not possess, which made me think that one, I need to work twice as hard as everyone else in order to achieve the same things that they achieve by default, but two, that I may just fucking fail because I'm not good enough. There were two key stories that I'm gonna share. Number one was when I was really young. I really wanted to make a particular sports team and I did everything. The entire summer, I worked my fucking tail off. I did extra training sessions. I did as much as I possibly could in order to put myself in the best possible position to make this team. And thankfully, for my situation, the kid that I was competing against decided, because he didn't really care about the sport, that he didn't want to play that season and I felt like all my hard work had paid off. But then, about four games into the season, this same kid, who didn't really give a fuck about the sport, decided that he did want to play and because this particular kid possessed raw ingredients or raw talents that I didn't have, I was promptly kicked out of the team and he was my replacement. I was replaced by someone who didn't really give a fuck, who didn't do any of the pre-season work that I did to get there. The second story is in my final year of high school. I had never really been a good student. I'd always struggled at school, but that particular year, something in me felt this need to actually give it a crack. And so I put my heart and soul into my art and into my English. I did endless practice essays. I spent endless hours working as hard as I possibly could to put myself in the best possible position to succeed. And it actually got to the point where I was beginning to succeed. In all of my tests throughout the year, I'd started to get better and better marks. I got some confidence. My teacher started to praise me for the work that I was doing. I felt like I was actually accomplishing things. And then my final exams came. I failed both subjects. I got the equivalent of about a 40 out of 100 in English and about a 32 out of 100 in art. I failed miserably. And so now the pattern that I'm recognizing in my own career is twofold. The first is that I have a tendency to make things more difficult than they need to be. I always choose the path of most resistance because I believe in myself that I have to work twice as hard as everyone else. In fact, much of the results that I have does not surprise me. I feel like I've earned everything I've earned because I try to outwork my competition I think there's a part of me that has my foot out the door. I think there's a small part of me that at any moment in time has one foot out the door because I am perpetually worried for that more talented kid to come and take my place. Or I'm perpetually worried that I'm just gonna get a surprise failure and I'm gonna have to come to grips with the fact that I put enormous amounts of effort into this. And so I think there's a small part of me who refuses to go all the way in because by not going all the way in, I have an excuse when it eventually fails. And so now the behavior that I'm trying to unlearn is the fear of being replaced by someone more talented and failing in spite of effort. I have to acknowledge that I don't need to work twice as hard as everyone else. I can choose the path of least resistance. I can lean on my strengths. I do have strengths. And if I don't put both feet in the arena, if I don't do the most that I possibly can, if I don't go all in, that will be the reason I don't succeed. Not because I lack the raw ingredients that I believe I don't have.
outliers.why_it_worked: Extreme vulnerability creates an immediate, deep connection. The specific story makes a universal fear of loss feel concrete, forcing the viewer to reflect on their own vulnerabilities.
outliers.headline: The Day I Lost Everything
outliers.recommended_angle: Frame the 'Alaska story' not just as a loss, but as the origin story for the entire 'systems beat hustle' philosophy. The trauma of losing every physical asset created an obsession with building digital, unbreakable systems.
my_hooks: In 2018, I went for a hike in Alaska. When I came back, everything I owned was gone.
I'm going to tell you about the worst day of my life. Because it explains everything about how I build businesses today.
I used to believe hard work was everything. A single car window taught me I was wrong.
A part of me is still terrified of losing it all again. Here's the system I built to fight that feeling.
This isn't a motivational story. It's about how a smashed window in a parking lot changed my entire life.
my_talking_points: I'm going to share something that I don't talk about a lot. It’s the origin of why I am so obsessed with systems. And maybe you’ll recognize the pattern in your own life.
In 2018, I spent four and a half months on a documentary project, driving from California to Alaska. My entire life was in my van.
One afternoon, I went for a hike. When I came back, my window was smashed. Everything was gone.
My cameras. My hard drives. Four months of work. Everything I had hustled for, just erased.

And in that silence, standing in that parking lot, I realized something. Hard work is fragile. Anything you build that depends on one location, one person, or one piece of glass can be taken from you in an instant.
That day created a pattern in me. An obsession with building things that can't be stolen. Things that aren't fragile.
You can steal my camera. You can't steal my customer acquisition system. You can smash my hard drive. You can't smash an AI employee that runs 24/7, booking calls while I sleep.
I stopped building things that required my hands and started building engines that run without me. That's the entire idea behind FieldWork.
It's about using AI and automation to build a business online, so you can live your life offline. Without the fear that one bad day can wipe it all out.
If you want to learn how to build a business that can't be smashed with a rock, comment the word 'FieldWork' below and I'll send you the details.
outliers.my_body_copy: I'm going to share the single moment that changed how I think about business forever. It's the reason I'm obsessed with systems. In 2018, I was on a four month documentary project in Alaska. My entire life was in my van. One afternoon, I went for a hike. I came back to a smashed window. Everything was gone. My camera gear. My hard drives with months of work. The physical result of all my hustle was just gone. And I realized hard work is fragile. Anything that depends on one piece of glass is a liability. That day, I became obsessed with building things that couldn't be stolen. You can steal a camera. You can't steal a customer acquisition system. You can smash a hard drive. You can't smash an AI employee that works while you sleep. I stopped building things and started building engines. That's the core of FieldWork. We build online systems so you can live your life offline, without the fear of losing it all. If you want to build a business that can't be smashed with a rock, comment 'FieldWork' below.
my_script_summary: Hook: The Alaska story. Went for a hike, came back to a smashed window.
The Loss: Everything was gone. Camera gear, hard drives, months of work.
The Realization: Hard work tied to physical things is fragile and can be erased instantly.
The Pivot: Became obsessed with building unbreakable, digital systems that can't be stolen.
The Contrast: A camera can be stolen. A customer acquisition system cannot.
The Offer: This philosophy is the foundation of FieldWork, building systems to live offline.
The CTA: Comment 'FieldWork' to learn how.
production_guidance: Film as a simple, direct-to-camera talking head. Maintain eye contact to build trust and intimacy.
If any photos or b-roll exist from the Alaska trip, use quick cuts to show the van, the landscape, or the gear.
Use bold on-screen text for key phrases: 'Everything was gone.', 'Hard work is fragile.', 'Systems beat hustle.'
End with a clear, full-screen text card: 'Comment "FieldWork"' for the final 3-5 seconds.
